item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: PickPik
License: CC0
Title: closeup photo of person holding bar soap
Author: N/A
Release year: N/A

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article was missing the

« SCP-1956 | SCP-1957 | SCP-1958 »

I added it, I hope that was okay.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Right. So the last proposal turned out to be a dude due to the linked to tale not being about SCP-1957. Lets try this again. SCP-2858 has a lot in common with SCP-1957, from both being anomalous soap, to unknown manufacturers. Its likely the Foundation would look into the manufacturer of one being the manufacturer of the other. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Due to SCP-1957's primarily being found within urban areas, it is currently theorized that it was deliberately created and exposed to human population centers. Research into groups potentially responsible for SCP-1957's creation is ongoing. As of 08/17/1978, SCP-1957 has been classified as Euclid.

To this:

Due to SCP-1957's primarily being found within urban areas, it is currently theorized that it was deliberately created and exposed to human population centers. Research into groups potentially responsible for SCP-1957's creation is ongoing. As of 08/17/1978, SCP-1957 has been classified as Euclid.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Thank you, I wasn't 100% sure what to do.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Raggabrash, perhaps you would want to highlight that to subletea or a member of Wikiwalk via PM.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: It may be a little late to say this, but Skin Deep is about SCP-1317, not 1957. I don't think the articles are connected.

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show proposed edits
- Hide proposed edits

This article has had a tale written about its origins, Skin Deep making it easy to find a connection between the two. As the tale is based around recovered documents, I have added instructions for viewing them in the containment procedures of the article.

Editing from this:

All instances of SCP-1957 are held in vacuum sealed containment lockers, located in Site-77. All containment lockers used in SCP-1957's containment must be waterproofed, and checked daily for the presence of moisture. Personnel assigned to study SCP-1957 are to be screened for the presence SCP-1957 flakes upon exiting the containment area.

Research into how SCP-1957 factored into the creation of the SCP-1837 anomaly is ongoing.

To this:

All instances of SCP-1957 are held in vacuum sealed containment lockers, located in Site-77. All containment lockers used in SCP-1957's containment must be waterproofed, and checked daily for the presence of moisture. Personnel assigned to study SCP-1957 are to be screened for the presence SCP-1957 flakes upon exiting the containment area.

The recovered notes of Doctor Nathan Farber are currently stored in a digital format in the main database of Site-77. Foundation personnel with Level Two clearance or higher who are currently assigned to SCP-1957 may access these notes at any time.

Research into how SCP-1957 factored into the creation of the SCP-1837 anomaly is ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What gets me about this one is the capitalization in the note, which I actually kinda liked unlike most people here. It feels mechanical, like it was made by someone or something that understands that capitalization can be used for dramatic effect but doesn't understand how to do it right, much like the object itself. It's like someone or something saw that dirty streets were an issue but doesn't quite understand how the world works so it decided that the best way to clean the streets was to make bars of soap that turn people into trash magnets. The soap doesn't kill you out of malice, it kills you because who or whatever made it has good intentions but doesn't understand what it's doing, like a child trying to repair his father's car. While the object itself isn't very interesting, who or what created it is what makes the article here. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the only unifying feature being the anomalous property they share. Each instance of SCP-1957 has the words "keep our streets clean!" embossed into its exterior

So, two unifying features.

Nit status: Picked

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this one is really weird and vaguely weak but theres something cool about the concept. upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, it kind of reminded me of the labels on Dr. Bonner's soaps. They get into some pretty odd capitalizations too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just strikes me as OTT soap with a cut-pasted conspiracy group tacked onto it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1957 designates a collection of 45 anomalous soaps.

I think that should be "anomalous bars of soap".

With repeated growth of more intense SCP-1957 instances, testing has shown that objects not normally considered to be refuse may be affected, such as vehicles, abandoned buildings, deceased organisms, and [REDACTED].

1) If the object being attracted towards the person is large enough, does the person get pulled towards the object? That is, does the anomaly respect "for every reaction, there's a equal and opposite reaction"?

2) I think the foundation would have a note about the maximum amount of force, in Newtons, that can be exerted on the objects.

Subjects may be crushed or suffocate to death due to the pressure created by these objects.

If the large object is accelerated with enough force, and is far enough away to accelerate for long enough, the force of impact could kill through blunt trauma.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find the concept pleasantly compelling, but the note really soured my view of the article. It read like it was trying to hard to sound crazy, especially given the capitalisations and ridiculousness of the hyperbole.

I'm not sure what it is, but I'm sure there's a much more compelling way to write the note than another time-cube-esque rant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alas, I must look elsewhere for my next muse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would just like to comment that for some reason I have become instantly enamoured with the phrase "anomalous soaps".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SPINNING CYCLONES OF FILTH and TEARS

Street sweepers shall forever be known to me by this name.

Anyways, I liked the twist; at first it seemed to be leaning towards the anomalous OCD soap cliche, but it turned around quite nicely. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the addition of a group attached to these folks. That little bit of fluff secured my upvote, though it wasn't hard. I originally thought it might be a soap-what-makes-you-OCD, but I was pleasantly blown away by the fuckin' weirdness of this thing.

Also, moral of the story—use Dr. Bronner's castile soap instead /s

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instances of SCP-1957 have varying colors and sizes, with the only unifying feature being the anomalous property they share. Each instance of SCP-1957 has the words "keep our streets clean!" embossed into its exterior.

The second line contradicts the first. Just a minor nitpick but it just bugged me.

Also, do they end up growing soap on them after the three weeks? That just seemed a bit weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …That document. I just… Fake conspiracy theories are right up there with propaganda for me after seeing the fluoride man. That's brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, not entirely sure how I feel about this one. I feel like I'm just not getting it. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have possibly robbed us of Memetickemu 2: The Electric Boogaloo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"An upper limit for this effect appears to correspond with the size of the body of water the subject was exposed within, with larger bodies of water increasing the intensity of exposure."

Note: Keep well away from the ocean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding the capitalization thing, because I really, really don't like it.

Other than that, I was really meh on this. It failed to really draw me in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
does it? Damn, then I must've fucked up somewhere with the ctrl+v'ing.

Oh well, wasn't that great anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That pastebin just has this SCP in it. Unless that's what you were going for… but I can't imagine why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this would be better if the random capitalization was removed from the attached document. Right now, the formatting draws attention away from the content of the letter, which is actually pretty strong.

Otherwise, this is good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was an error on my part, fix'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. I get the feeling that there's something here I'm not getting, but it is good and seems genuinely creepy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These instances will retain the intensity of their parent instances effect. With repeated growth of more intense SCP-1957 instances…

The first sentence says that the new soaps are as strong as their parents, if I'm reading it right. The second says that the new ones are stronger.

I'm neutral about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Found an unsaved draft when I was posting this. I would show it to you, but I pasted the wrong thing. Now it is lost to history.
